content:
A deep dive into the first stage of every card payment.

Most of us have made a card payment and seen the familiar message:

Transaction Successful

The entire process takes only a few seconds.

From the customer's perspective, that's the end of the story.

Behind the scenes, however, that approval is the result of a distributed system involving merchants, payment providers, payment switches, card schemes, issuing banks, fraud detection systems, and multiple network hops.

Before any money moves, one important question must be answered:

Should this payment be approved?

That question is answered during Authorization.

In this article, we'll explore what authorization is, why banks place authorization holds, and how this stage fits into the overall card payment lifecycle.

The Payment Lifecycle

Every successful card payment follows a sequence of events.

Customer
‚îÇ
‚ñº
Authorization
‚îÇ
‚ñº
Clearing
‚îÇ
‚ñº
Settlement
‚îÇ
‚ñº
Payout

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

This article focuses on the first stage.

Authorization.

What is Authorization?

Authorization is the process of deciding whether a payment should be allowed to continue.

It is not the movement of money.

It is simply a decision.

When you tap your debit card at a POS terminal or click Pay Now on an online checkout page, a payment request begins its journey.

The request eventually reaches the bank that issued your card.

Before approving it, the issuing bank performs several checks.

What Does the Bank Check?

An issuing bank doesn't blindly approve every transaction.

It typically verifies:

‚Ä¢ Is the card valid?

‚Ä¢ Has the card expired?

‚Ä¢ Is the card blocked or reported stolen?

‚Ä¢ Is there enough available balance or credit?

‚Ä¢ Does the transaction trigger fraud detection rules?

‚Ä¢ Does the transaction exceed spending limits?

‚Ä¢ Does it require additional authentication?

If any of these checks fail, the transaction is declined.

If they all pass, the transaction is approved.

A Typical Authorization Flow

Imagine buying groceries worth ‚Ç¶25,000 using your Access Bank debit card at a supermarket.

The supermarket uses Moniepoint as its acquiring provider.

The payment journey looks something like this.

Customer
‚îÇ
‚ñº
POS Terminal
‚îÇ
‚ñº
Acquirer (Moniepoint)
‚îÇ
‚ñº
Payment Switch
‚îÇ
‚ñº
Issuer (Access Bank)

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

Access Bank evaluates the transaction.

If everything looks good, it returns an approval response.

That response follows the same path back to the POS terminal.

Access Bank
‚îÇ
‚ñº
Payment Switch
‚îÇ
‚ñº
Moniepoint
‚îÇ
‚ñº
POS Terminal
‚îÇ
‚ñº
Transaction Successful

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

All of this typically happens in under three seconds.

Authorization Doesn't Move Money

This is one of the biggest misconceptions about card payments.

Many people assume that once the POS says:

Transaction Successful

the merchant has already received the money.

Not quite.

Authorization simply means:

The bank has approved the payment.

The actual transfer of funds happens later during settlement.

What is an Authorization Hold?

After approving a transaction, the bank usually places a hold on the required amount.

This is known as an authorization hold.

The money hasn't left your account yet.

Instead, it has been reserved.

You can't spend it somewhere else.

Think of it as reserving a seat at the cinema.

The seat still exists.

It simply can't be taken by another customer.

The same idea applies to your money.

The funds remain in your account but are no longer available for other purchases.

Why Do Banks Place Holds?

Authorization holds solve an important problem.

Imagine this scenario.

You have ‚Ç¶30,000 in your account.

You make a purchase for ‚Ç¶25,000.

If the bank immediately approved the payment without reserving the money, you could spend the remaining balance before settlement occurs.

By the time settlement begins, the money might no longer be available.

The hold prevents that from happening.

It guarantees that the approved funds remain available until settlement.

Where You See Authorization Holds

Most people experience authorization holds without realizing it.

Hotels

Hotels often place a larger authorization hold during check-in.

The hold covers your room charges and any additional expenses.

When you check out, the final amount is settled, and any unused funds are released.

Fuel Stations

Some fuel stations authorize a higher amount before dispensing fuel.

If the final purchase is lower, the remaining hold is removed after settlement.

Car Rentals

Rental companies commonly authorize a security deposit before releasing a vehicle.

The final charge is settled when the vehicle is returned.

Why Authorization Matters

Authorization protects everyone involved in a payment.

For customers, it helps prevent fraudulent transactions.

For merchants, it confirms that the customer can pay.

For banks, it provides an opportunity to apply fraud detection, spending controls, and risk management before money moves.

Without authorization, payment systems would become significantly less secure.

Putting Everything Together

Here's a simplified view of the authorization process.

Customer taps card
‚îÇ
‚ñº
Merchant
‚îÇ
‚ñº
Acquirer
‚îÇ
‚ñº
Payment Switch
‚îÇ
‚ñº
Issuing Bank
‚îÇ
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Validate card
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Check balance
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Fraud checks
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Spending limits
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ Approve or Decline
‚îÇ
‚ñº
Funds placed on Hold
‚îÇ
‚ñº
Approval returned to Merchant

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

At this point:

The transaction has been approved.

The merchant has not been paid yet.

The payment lifecycle is only beginning.

What's Next?

Authorization is only the first stage.

The next step is Clearing.

This is where financial institutions exchange transaction records and agree on exactly what needs to be settled before any money moves.

We'll explore that in the next article.

Final Thoughts

Every time you tap your card, dozens of systems work together in a matter of seconds.

Before you ever see "Transaction Successful," your bank has already validated your card, checked your balance, evaluated fraud risk, and reserved the required funds.

That's authorization.

It may be invisible to the customer, but it's one of the most important stages in every card payment.
